senses.definition: A decorative flap or piece of fabric covering the front opening of trousers, historically used in certain garments.
examples: The 16th-century doublet featured a prominent braguette as part of its design.
Costume historians note the braguette as a functional and ornamental element of traditional dress.

senses.definition: A word or phrase used to boast or show off; a term associated with bragging language.
examples: The author coined bragworts to describe self-promoting expressions in literature.
In slang, bragwort can refer to boastful terms used to inflate one’s image.

senses.definition: A variant spelling for Brahmā or Brahman in Hindu theology, referring to the ultimate reality or a sacred concept; can also be a shorthand for Brahmana or Brahmin in some contexts.
examples: In some texts, 'Brahm' is used to denote the supreme reality beyond form.
The name Brahm appears in certain Sanskrit-derived names and terms.

senses.definition: A Hindu god of creation and the Vedas; the creator aspect in the Trimurti; also used to refer to the universal spiritual reality (Brahman) in philosophical contexts.
examples: Brahma is traditionally depicted with four heads as the creator.
In Vedantic thought, Brahma and Brahman are related concepts referring to ultimate reality.

senses.definition: A Hindu or Jain student who has taken a vow of celibacy and is focused on spiritual learning.
examples: A brahmachari renounces worldly life to pursue spiritual discipline.
The ashram hosts several brahmacharis who study sacred texts.

senses.definition: The state or quality of Brahma as the creator; used metaphorically to denote a supreme or all-encompassing creative power.
examples: The temple was dedicated to brahmahood, emphasizing cosmic creation.
In poetic language, brahmahood can symbolize ultimate origin and order.

senses.definition: Relating to Brahma or to the concept of Brahman; divine or cosmic in nature.
examples: The temple’s brahmaic imagery conveyed a sense of universal creation.
They spoke in brahmaic terms about the origin of the universe.

senses.definition: A member of the priestly caste in Hindu society; or the universal spiritual essence in Hindu philosophy (Brahman).
examples: A brahman performs the sacred rites with precise recitation.
In Hinduism, Brahman is the ultimate reality that underlies all existence.

senses.definition: A member of the Brahmana caste or a priestly scholar in Vedic society; also refers to a class of texts (Brahmanas) associated with the Vedas.
examples: Brahmanas are prose texts that explain the rituals of the Vedas.
A brahmana is often learned in the sacred laws and commentaries.

senses.definition: A Vedic deity or divine figure associated with wealth, prosperity, and eloquence; sometimes identified with Sarasvati or other divine beings in certain texts.
examples: Brahmanaspati is invoked for wisdom and eloquence in rituals.
Hymns mention brahmanaspati as a supporter of learning.

senses.definition: In Hindu cosmology, the cosmic egg or the universe as a whole; the Brahmanda.
examples: The brahmanda is described as the source of all creation.
Cosmological myths often refer to the brahmanda as a boundless cosmic sphere.

senses.definition: A female member of the Brahmin or Brahmana class, or a woman embodying Brahmanic qualities; usage is very rare and archaic.
examples: In some texts, brahmaness denotes a learned or virtuous Brahmin woman.
The term brahmaness appears infrequently in classical literature.

senses.definition: The state or quality of being a Brahmin or a Brahminical person; can refer to caste status or the associated cultural duties.
examples: Brahanhood carried responsibilities and rites in ancient communities.
Some texts discuss brahmanhood as a path of learning and service.

senses.definition: A female member of the Brahmin class; feminine form used in some literary or traditional contexts.
examples: The brahmani contributed to scholarly discourse in the ashram.
Texts sometimes refer to brahmani as a learned priestess.

senses.definition: Relating to Brahman or Brahmins; characteristic of Brahmanic culture or thought.
examples: The sermon reflected brahmanic ideas about duty and knowledge.
The temple’s brahmanic rituals emphasized ritual purity.

senses.definition: Of or relating to Brahmanism or Brahmanical tradition; often used to describe social, religious, or cultural aspects associated with Brahmins.
examples: Brahmanical norms shaped many village rituals.
The essay examined brahmanical influence on temple architecture.

senses.definition: Plural of brahmani or a reference to Brahmins in some linguistic variants; used in historical or religious contexts.
examples: The brahmanis gathered for the ritual readings.
Ancient manuscripts mention several brahmanis who tutored teachers.

senses.definition: A system of religious beliefs associated with Brahmins; emphasis on ritual knowledge and Vedic tradition.
examples: Brahmanism influenced temple rites and Vedic study.
Historical texts discuss the rise of Brahmanism in the region.

senses.definition: Plural of brahmanism; various schools or expressions within the Brahminical tradition.
examples: The book compares several brahmanisms across regions.
Scholars debate differences among brahmanisms.

senses.definition: A follower or advocate of Brahmanism; a person who upholds Brahminical traditions.
examples: The brahmanist argued for the primacy of ritual purity.
Brahmanists societies existed in several ancient towns.

senses.definition: Relating to Brahmanism or Brahmanistic practices; dominated by ritual knowledge and priestly culture.
examples: A brahmanistic code governed village ceremonies.
The manuscript describes brahmanistic rites in detail.

senses.definition: Plural of brahmanist; adherents or practitioners of Brahmanism.
examples: Brahmanists attended the ceremony to honor the elder's memory.
Historically, brahmanists held influence in temple colleges.

senses.definition: To make something more Brahmanic or to convert to Brahmanism; to introduce ritual or priestly elements into a place or practice.
examples: The temple was brahmanized with new priestly rituals.
Efforts to brahmanize schooling focused on Vedic study.

senses.definition: Plural of brahman; members of the Brahmin caste or priests in Hindu society.
examples: Brahmans historically held roles as teachers and priests.
Several brahmans participated in the ceremony.

senses.definition: An archaic or literary term referring to Brahmins or Brahmanic people; usage is rare in modern English.
examples: Historical texts mention brahmany as respected scholars.
The term brahmany appears in old religious chronicles.
